title: Lesson Quiz: Basics of Taxes and Financial Responsibility
description: Assessment of the concept that Purpose of taxes, common types, and obligations.

questions.prompt: What is one purpose of taxes in basic personal finance?

choices.text: To help fund public services and obligations
choices.feedback: Correct. Taxes are required payments that help support public responsibilities and services.
Taxes do not increase personal spending power.

choices.text: To remove every deduction from a paycheck
choices.feedback: Taxes may be one type of deduction, but they do not remove all deductions.

choices.text: To eliminate the need for budgeting
choices.feedback: Budgeting is still important because taxes affect real costs and take-home pay.

questions.prompt: Sales tax can increase the final cost of a purchase above the listed price.

choices.feedback: Sales tax can raise the final total above the listed pre-tax price.
Correct. The final amount paid can be higher after sales tax is added.

questions.prompt: An item costs $45 and sales tax is $3.60. What is the total cost?

choices.feedback: The listed price and the sales tax need to be added fully.
This total is too high. Add the listed price and tax carefully.
$49.50 is too high for this example.

questions.prompt: Why is tax awareness part of financial responsibility?

choices.text: Because taxes affect real financial outcomes and should be included in planning
choices.feedback: This is strong reasoning, but the correct answer in this set more clearly connects taxes to real comparisons and final costs.

choices.text: Because taxes never affect earnings or prices
choices.feedback: Taxes can affect paychecks and purchase totals.

choices.text: Because taxes should be considered when comparing what money is actually available or what a purchase will really cost
choices.feedback: Correct. Financial responsibility includes comparing actual after-tax results.

choices.text: Because taxes only matter after all decisions are made
choices.feedback: Taxes should be considered before making financial decisions.

questions.prompt: Using only a listed pre-tax price can lead to a weak financial decision.

choices.feedback: Correct. A person should consider after-tax cost, not just the listed pre-tax amount.
Ignoring tax can lead to an inaccurate view of the final cost.
